head_coach.name: Kevin Stefanski
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Wide-zone run-first offense rooted in Kubiak/Shanahan tree; play-action heavy, multiple tight end usage, committed to marrying run and pass

offensive_coordinator.name: Tommy Rees
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Stefanski/Kubiak wide zone; college background at Notre Dame and Alabama as OC

defensive_coordinator.name: Jeff Ulbrich
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Robert Saleh/Pete Carroll Seattle 4-3 base evolved into hybrid multiple front; heavy man coverage influence from Jets tenure

offense.scheme_family: Kubiak/Shanahan wide zone with gap scheme complements
offense.personnel_notes: Falcons used league-high 12 personnel rate in 2025 (87.3% in some games); Stefanski historically favors 12 and even 13 personnel packages. Expect continued heavy 12 personnel with Kyle Pitts and a blocking TE, blending into 11 personnel as matchups dictate.
offense.pass_concepts: play action heavy off wide zone, boot concepts, deep crossers off play action, coverage indicators via motion and TE shifts, quick game layups for QB
offense.rpo_usage: low to moderate; Stefanski prefers traditional play action over RPOs
offense.te_usage: critical to scheme — dual TE sets with Y (inline blocker) and F (move TE/flex). Kyle Pitts used inline, slot, and out wide. Pitts led team in receptions (88) and receiving yards (928) in 2025, earning All-Pro honors. Stefanski has historically deployed TEs as both run-game anchors and primary receiving weapons.
offense.rb_usage: bell cow with significant receiving role. Bijan Robinson is the offensive centerpiece — 1,478 rushing yards plus elite receiving production (4th-best PFF receiving grade among HBs at 88.1). Robinson had 274 receiving yards through 4 weeks in 2025 and eclipsed 2,000 scrimmage yards.

defense.alignment: primarily even front (4-down) but shifts to odd front looks; Ulbrich described scheme as '3-4-ish and 4-3-ish at times' with multiplicity as a core principle. His Jets tenure was rooted in Saleh's 4-3 under scheme. In Atlanta, he emphasizes multiple fronts to counter modern offenses.
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-3 DE / stand-up hybrid. James Pearce Jr. (10.5 sacks as rookie) primarily hands-in-dirt DE; Jalon Walker (5.5 sacks) hybrid EDGE/LB who was placed in edge rushers room but has LB versatility. Team blitzed at 2nd-highest rate in NFL in 2025.
defense.coverage_base: More man-heavy than predecessor. Jets under Ulbrich ran Cover 1 at 28.6% (7th in NFL) vs. Atlanta's prior 11.3%. Expect significant increase in man coverage, blended with Cover 3 zone. Multiple coverage shell approach.
defense.blitz_tendency: heavy (2nd-highest blitz rate in NFL in 2025). Ulbrich's attacking philosophy generated franchise-record 57 sacks.
defense.lb_role: hybrid — ILBs (Kaden Elliss) used in coverage and as occasional blitzers; Walker drafted as chess piece who plays edge primarily but projects to SAM/overhang role on early downs with pass rush duties on third down. Ulbrich values versatile LBs who can rush and cover.
defense.safety_usage: Jessie Bates III plays deep free safety role (All-Pro 2025); Xavier Watts (5 INTs as rookie) and Billy Bowman Jr. provide depth. Safeties used in Cover 1 / Cover 3 shell. Box safety usage moderate — Ulbrich prefers to generate pressure from front seven and LBs rather than heavy safety blitzes.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: This is one of the highest TE-value offenses in the NFL. Stefanski's scheme is built on 12 personnel and multiple TE sets — he led the league in 13 personnel usage in Cleveland and Atlanta ran league-high 12 personnel rates in 2025. Kyle Pitts earned All-Pro honors with 88 receptions and 928 yards as essentially the team's top pass-catcher from the TE position. Stefanski explicitly distinguishes between Y (inline) and F (move) TEs, deploying the F as a flexed route-runner. Pitts is used inline, in the slot, and split wide. A receiving TE with elite route-running and separation ability is valued like a WR1 in this offense.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Ulbrich's defense blitzed at the 2nd-highest rate in the NFL in 2025 and produced a franchise-record 57 sacks. The Falcons drafted Jalon Walker 15th overall specifically as a hybrid LB/EDGE chess piece — he was placed in the edge rushers room but has LB coverage ability. Ulbrich's scheme values versatile front-seven players who can rush from multiple alignments. With the multiple front concept shifting between 3-4 and 4-3 looks, an LB with elite pass rush traits (burst, bend, first step) gets significant value as a stand-up rusher, SAM blitzer, and edge-setting defender. Walker's archetype — undersized EDGE/LB hybrid — is precisely what this defense covets.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Bijan Robinson is the prototype for this offense — an elite runner who is also a top-4 receiving back in the NFL (PFF receiving grade 88.1). Robinson's 274 receiving yards through 4 weeks, 2,000+ scrimmage yards, and WR1-type receiving stretches (15 catches for 174 yards and a TD over two late-season games) demonstrate how Stefanski's wide-zone play-action scheme creates massive receiving volume for the RB. The Kubiak/Shanahan tree offense uses the RB in the flat, on screen concepts, and as a check-down option off play action. A dual-threat RB with elite hands and route-running gets a significant value boost.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Ulbrich's defense is primarily a single-high or two-deep shell that generates pressure from the front seven and LBs rather than through exotic safety blitzes. Jessie Bates III operates as a traditional deep free safety. Xavier Watts showed ball-hawking ability (5 INTs) in a more conventional role. However, the high man-coverage rate (Ulbrich's Jets ran 32.5% man) does create value for safeties who can match up in the slot and play physical man coverage near the line. A safety with slot coverage and tackling versatility has moderate value, but this is not a scheme that primarily creates hybrid safety roles like a Vic Fangio defense would.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Atlanta under Stefanski is a premier landing spot for receiving TEs — the 12-personnel-heavy, Kubiak-tree offense makes an elite pass-catching TE function as the de facto WR1 (Kyle Pitts had 88 catches, 928 yards in 2025). A prospect like Kenyon Sadiq would see his value spike here. On defense, Ulbrich's multiple front with the NFL's 2nd-highest blitz rate and franchise-record 57 sacks in 2025 creates enormous value for hybrid LB/EDGE prospects. The Jalon Walker archetype (undersized, explosive, versatile) is exactly what this defense wants — LBs with pass-rush traits should be valued near EDGE prices for ATL. The RB position carries elite dual-threat value: Stefanski's scheme created 2,000+ scrimmage-yard production for Bijan Robinson with significant receiving volume. Any RB prospect with route-running and hands gets a meaningful boost. EDGE remains a critical need heading into 2026 with Pearce's legal issues and multiple free agents departing, making pass rushers of all types — traditional DE and hybrid LB — high-priority targets.
